data.title: Surprise, Surprise: Two More REIT Buys
data.description:
This continues to be a good environment for investing in REITs. REITs do well when interest rates are stable or falling, and inflation is low.

Over 75% of investors expect at least one rate cut in the next 12 months, bringing the prime rate down to 4.25%.

Meanwhile, YoY (year-over-year) inflation, at 3.3%, is running below the no-risk rate on Treasuries. The 10-year note is yielding 4.5%, while the 2-year pays 4.3%. A retiree living on dividends can sit in Treasuries at no risk, and inch ahead slightly.

According to a note by Citigroup from December, REITs are expected to return 10% to 15% in 2025, helped by a favorable environment. If Citigroup is right, that 10-15% return would be more than double the no-risk rate, making REITs worth the gamble, particularly if you choose well.

Healthcare REITs and Industrials are off to a fast start this year.

Industrials are enjoying a rotation after a 15% selloff in 2024, despite stellar operating performance. Prologis, the leading company in this sector, has seen "an impressive acceleration in leasing activity following the U.S. election," and believes the industrials market is bottoming. Meanwhile, absorption (demand) is finally catching up to completions (supply), following a 2-year glut.

Healthcare REITs are being boosted by both increased income (Cost of Living Adjustments) for their senior housing and skilled nursing patients, and a 15-year low in construction starts on new facilities. As Hoya Capital noted recently,

Fueled by tailwinds from record-setting COLA adjustments in 2023 and 2024, rent growth across both Senior Housing and Skilled Nursing facilities remained historically strong in 2024, each rising by over 4%. Supply growth remains muted as well, with NIC noting that the rolling four-quarter average for construction starts sits at 1.0% of total inventory - the lowest since 2010.

MOBs (medical office buildings) have remained fundamentally sound while enduring 6 consecutive quarters in which completions outpaced absorption, but that trend has begun reversing, starting in Q2 2024.

This article highlights two well-positioned companies to buy right now: one an Industrial, the other a Healthcare REIT.

STAG Industrial

STAG Industrial (STAG) owns 591 warehouse facilities, totaling over $115 msf (million square feet), spread across 41 U.S. states, including all Tier 1 industrial real estate markets. The company is headquartered in Boston and went public as a REIT in 2011. Leasing spreads over the past 12 months have been in excess of 27%.

Only 20% of the U.S. construction pipeline is located in STAG's top 10 markets. While the average new facility under construction is .30 msf, the average STAG facility is less than half that size. So the supply picture is tilted in the company's favor.

Meanwhile, E-commerce continues to increase as a percentage of all retail sales, driving demand for warehouse space.

STAG Growth

Here are the 3-year growth figures for FFO (funds from operations) and TCFO (total cash from operations).

Metric 2021 2022 2023 2024 3-year CAGR
FFO (millions) $332 $401 $421 $458 --
FFO Growth % -- 20.8 5.0 8.8 11.32%
FFO per share $2.06 $2.40 $2.21 $2.48 --
FFO per share growth % -- 16.5 (-8.0) 12.2 6.38%
TCFO (millions) $336 $388 $391 $460 --
TCFO Growth % -- 15.5 0.8 17.6 11.04%

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium and author calculations

As you can see, STAG has consistently grown FFO and TCFO by an average of over 11.0%, which is pretty impressive. FFO/share growth comes in at a healthy but not spectacular 6.38%, due to a step back in 2023.

Management is guiding for same-store NOI growth of 5.25–5.50% in 2025. The company expects to make $500 - $700 million in acquisitions, at a healthy cap rate of 6.25–6.50%, and $100 - $150 million in dispositions.

Meanwhile, here is how the stock price has done over the past 3 twelve-month periods, compared to the REIT average as represented by the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ).

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-yr CAGR
STAG share price Feb. 17 $39.30 $35.46 $39.10 $35.06 --
STAG share price Gain % -- (-9.8) 10.3 (-10.3) (-3.73)%
VNQ share price Feb. 17 $102.63 $89.17 $84.37 $91.63 --
VNQ share price Gain % -- (-13.1) (-5.4) 8.6 (-3.71)%

Source: MarketWatch.com and author calculations

Although the VNQ and STAG have gone in opposite directions each of the past two years, their 3-year CAGR is almost identical, at 3.7%. Factoring in the yield, STAG investors and VNQ investors have basically just broken even over the past 36 months.

STAG Balance sheet

Here are the key balance sheet metrics.

Company Liquidity Ratio Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA Bond Rating
STAG 2.07 32% 5.3 BBB

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Schwab, and author calculations

With a sturdy liquidity ratio of 2.07, debt ratio of 32%, and debt/EBITDA ratio of 5.3, STAG's bond-rated balance sheet is in very good shape.

As of December 31, STAG had $36.3 million in cash on hand, and a total of $2.85 billion in long-term debt, held at a weighted average interest rate of 3.97%, with a weighted average maturity of 4.6 years.

Less than 10% of STAG's debt matures in 2025 and 2027, but more than 10% in 2026, and a formidable wall forms in 2028 and 2029.

STAG Dividend

Among Industrial REITs, only two out-yield STAG, yet the company's Dividend Safety rating from Seeking Alpha Premium is a rock-solid B. The 4.25% dividend yield for STAG is highly competitive with the no-risk rate on Treasuries.

Company Div. Yield 3-yr Div. Growth Div. Score Payout Div. Safety
STAG 4.25% 0.68% 4.34 60% B

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Dividend Score projects the Yield three years from now, on shares bought today, assuming the Dividend Growth rate remains unchanged.

STAG Valuation

Despite its relatively high dividend yield, solid growth prospects, and solid balance sheet, STAG is selling at just 14.1x Price/FFO '24, well below both the Industrial REIT and overall REIT averages.

Company Div. Score Price/FFO '24 Premium to NAV
STAG 4.34 14.1 (-12.3)%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, and author calculations

Bottom line: STAG

STAG is a prosperous company, striking a wonderful balance between double-digit growth on the one hand, and highly competitive yield on the other. With a yield that almost matches the no-risk rate on Treasuries, sturdy growth prospects, and a strong balance sheet, all at a nice price, STAG offers a good chance to earn some capital gains at very low risk.

Five Wall Street analysts rate STAG a Buy or Strong Buy, and none rate the company a Sell. The average price target is $38.92, implying 10.8% upside. Zacks rates STAG a Sell, however, and the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings system recently downgraded it from Buy to Hold, based on negative earnings revisions.

Omega Healthcare Investors

Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) provides triple-net leasing and fixed-rate mortgage loans for 86 skilled nursing and assisted living facilities in 41 U.S. states and the U.K., with 1000 properties totaling over 92,000 beds, all under third-party management.

The company is headquartered in Maryland, and went public in 1992. SNFs (skilled nursing facilities) comprise 70% of OHI's portfolio, while senior housing makes up the other 30%. Assets are most concentrated in the Sun Belt and up the Mississippi and Ohio River valleys into Pennsylvania.

In addition to being the most likely destination for seniors discharged from the hospital, SNFs are also the lowest-cost option for 24-hour nursing care.

Importantly, as the U.S. population ages and demand for SNF's increases, the supply has remained unchanged for many years, due mostly to regulatory restrictions. This puts upward pressure on occupancy rates. Currently, OHI's SNF portfolio enjoys only 80.9% occupancy. This should also rise as more and more Baby Boomers join the 75-or-older cohort.

Omega is quite a bit more adept than the average REIT at converting revenue into EBITDA.

In addition to being triple-net leases, the company's leases are also long-term, with an average term of 9.4 years, very few near-term expirations, and very low risk of lease turnover.

OHI's tenant diversification is pretty good. The top 2 tenants each account for 10% of ABR, but the top 10 together only account for 58%.

OHI Growth

Here are the 3-year growth figures for FFO (funds from operations) and TCFO (total cash from operations).

Metric 2021 2022 2023 2024 3-year CAGR
FFO (millions) $655 $460 $591 $734 --
FFO Growth % -- (-29.8) 28.5 24.2 3.87%
FFO per share $3.31 $2.71 $2.99 $2.88 --
FFO per share growth % -- (-18.1) 10.3 (-3.7) (-4.53)%
TCFO (millions) $722 $626 $618 $749 --
TCFO Growth % -- (-13.3) (-1.3) 21.2 1.23%

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

According to the company, despite its high dividend yield, OHI has managed to grow AFFO by an average annual rate of 6.5% since 2004. If that is indeed the case, then most of that growth must have happened more than 5 years ago. 2021 was a banner year for the company, but since then, 3-year CAGR for FFO has been sluggish at 3.87%, although it is notable that the last two years, that same growth rate has been in excess of 20%. Cash from operations has grown at just a 1.23% clip. Meanwhile, FFO per share has stepped backward by an average of (-4.53)% annually.

Meanwhile, here is how the stock price has done over the past 3 twelve-month periods, compared to the REIT average as represented by the Vanguard Real Estate ETF.

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-yr CAGR
OHI share price Feb. 17 $28.82 $28.23 $30.95 $36.40 --
OHI share price Gain % -- (-2.0) 9.6 17.6 8.09%
VNQ share price Feb. 17 102.63 89.17 84.37 91.63 --
VNQ share price Gain % -- (-13.1) (-5.4) 8.6 (-3.71)%

Source: MarketWatch.com and author calculations

OHI shares are riding a hot streak that has seen the share price spike over 25% in the past 24 months. Adding in the dividend yield, OHI investors have enjoyed total returns in the neighborhood of 15% per annum.

OHI Balance sheet

Here are the key balance sheet metrics. Liquidity and debt ratio are solid, and the balance sheet is bond rated. Debt/EBITDA is outstanding, at 4.7.

Company Liquidity Ratio Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA Bond Rating
OHI 1.92 32% 4.7 BBB-

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Of the company's $4.44 billion in debt, 5.1% is held at variable rates. OHI has about $1.44 billion available on its revolving line of credit. About 8% of OHI debt matures in 2025. The following two years, 2026 and 2027, present a formidable wall of debt, with 25% of the total load due in 2027 alone.

OHI Dividend

OHI's dividend yield of 7.36% stands out from the crowd, and the lack of dividend growth is not a concern. However, the company earns a Dividend Safety grade of D from Seeking Alpha Quant ratings, due almost entirely to its aggressive payout ratios. However, their payout ratios have historically been high, and their revenue streams so stable and predictable that I think the dividend is safer than the Quant grade would indicate.

Company Div. Yield 3-yr Div. Growth Div. Score Payout Div. Safety
OHI 7.36% 0.0% 7.36 90% D

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Dividend Score projects the Yield three years from now, on shares bought today, assuming the Dividend Growth rate remains unchanged.

OHI Valuation

OHI shares are selling far below the REIT average and Healthcare sector average, at 12.6x. Although Hoya Capital shows share selling at a 47% premium to NAV, the Healthcare sector average is much higher.

Company Div. Score Price/FFO '24 Premium to NAV
OHI 7.36 12.6 47.0%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital and author calculations

Bottom line: OHI

With a yield almost double the no-risk rate, and positive growth prospects, OHI seems like a good bet to reward investors who are willing to run a small risk on the share price over the next year or two.

Seven Wall Street analysts rate OHI a Buy or Strong Buy, and none rate it a Sell. Zacks and the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings system also name OHI a Buy.

The average Wall Street price target on OHI is $41.87, implying 15.2% upside.

data.title: Haemonetics: Almost Blood In The Streets
data.description:
In the spring of last year, I believed that appeal was improving, following another bolt-on deal in the case of Haemonetics (NYSE:HAE). The blood business has seen modest growth ever since, but shares have fallen, mostly in the wake of a recently announced reduction in the full year sales guidance.

This move has increased appeal quite a bit, making current levels start to look a lot more compelling to get involved.

A Blood Business

Haemonetics describes itself as an innovative medical technology solution provider, aiming to improve quality, effectiveness and efficiency of care. The company generates about $1.3 billion in annual sales with more than 3,000 team members.

The largest business is the plasma business, responsible for nearly half of sales from disposables, collection devices and donor management services. The hospital segment makes up about a third of sales, comprised out of interventional and blood management technologies. This is complemented by the blood center segment, the third and smallest segment of the business.

Some three quarters of the $1.3 billion revenue base are generated in the US, complemented by about a 12% contribution from Europe, with the remainder of sales generated in the wider Asian region. Revenues have come in at nearly a billion between 2015 and 2022, but amidst inflation, organic growth and M&A efforts, sales have advanced to $1.3 billion.

Following a $253 million deal for Canada-based medical device cardiology business OpSens in the fall of 2023, the company was on track to earn close to $4 per share on an adjusted basis for all of 2023. With shares trading at $80 in the spring of last year, expectations remained elevated at around 20 times earnings, while net debt of $836 million resulted in a leverage ratio around 3 times.

While organic growth improved, I was somewhat cautious as some of the business units had suboptimal positioning, with not all activities focused on growth engines yet.

Expectations Come In

In May of last year, the company reported a 12% increase in fiscal 2024 sales to $1.31 billion. The company posted adjusted earnings of $3.96 per share, with GAAP earnings reported at just $2.29 per share after no less than 12 adjustments. While I am happy to adjust for amortization costs, and some others, I pegged realistic earnings at just $3 and change per share.

The company provided somewhat of a comforting outlook for the fiscal year 2025, with total sales growth seen between 5-8%, with M&A efforts contributing about five points, and organic growth seen coming in between flat and positive 3%. This should result in earnings per share of around $4.60 per share, plus or minus fifteen cents.

During the fall, the company posted sound second quarter results, and while the full-year guidance was kept intact, it was supported by stronger organic growth, offset by weaker contribution from M&A efforts. By December, the company announced the divestment of the whole blood assets business to GVS S.p.A. in a deal valued at $67 million, of which roughly two-thirds upfront and the remainder due in earn-outs in the coming years.

Early in February, Haemonetics reported a 4% increase in third quarter sales to $349 million, as organic growth came in flat. Total revenue growth for the year was cut to 3-5% amidst intensifying currency headwinds, a 3-4% contribution from acquisitions (a point less than the previous guidance), with organic growth seen at 0-3% (also a point less than the previous guidance).

While the company maintained the midpoint of the full-year earnings guidance at $4.60 per share, the midpoint of the free cash flow guidance was cut by twenty-five million to $130 million, equal to just over $2.50 per share. Net debt is reported at $904 million, as leverage ticked up amidst poor net working capital generation, and share buybacks.

Coming Down

Following the pullback in the sales guidance and free cash flow guidance, shares of Haemonetics have fallen to $64 per share, as shares have largely traded in a $70-$90 range over the past year. This values the business at $3.3 billion, or $4.2 billion if we factor in net debt.

Based on a $1.4 billion revenue base, this values the company at around 3 times sales. Adjusted earnings multiples have fallen from 20 times in the spring of last year to just about 14 times, but these earnings are somewhat adjusted, of course.

Even if I see realistic earnings at $3.50-$4.00 per share, these shares traded at a reasonable 16-18 times earnings multiple with net debt down to 2.4 times EBITDA. This debt load will come down minimally following the divestment of Whole Blood, which involves some $49 million in revenues leaving the door, but is not having an impact on earnings per share.

Putting this into perspective, Haemonetics was a $40 stock already a decade ago, and with exception to volatility in a $100-$150 range in the pandemic era, shares have fallen quite a bit, trading at historically very reasonable levels.

Weighing this altogether, the company is undergoing a reasonable transition, although organic growth in recent times has been underwhelming. That said, modest advancements in the business, but moreover share price declines, have resulted in multiples becoming a lot more appealing here, with appeal readily arising here.

data.title: iQIYI: Struggling Against Mini-Drama Surge And Macro Headwinds
data.description:
iQIYI (NASDAQ:IQ) reported its 4Q24 results, with the topline missing the consensus estimate on the back of a very low expectation. The most concerning part is that the deterioration of fundamentals is evident in both membership and advertising segments. Membership revenue declined for the fifth consecutive quarter due to a lack of hit content, while advertising remains pressured amid China’s challenging macro backdrop.

Management continues to remain optimistic, citing the recovery in membership revenue in 1Q25 due to a better content slate, but we remain cautious as we believe competition from mini-drama is eroding IQ’s subscriber base and that lack of innovation in content development to counter the emerging popularity of mini-drama could pose a key risk to IQ in 2025.

Within the online media vertical, our preference is Netflix over IQ as we believe NFLX has a more established self-reinforcing model and flywheel effect that allows it to gain global scalability.

We value IQ at 0.8x EV/Revenue, in line with content producer Paramount, which trades at a similar valuation range. This would imply 6.5x forward earnings, in line with that of ad peer Weibo, which trades at this range, given IQ’s exposure to China’s ad spending environment. We see another 16% downside from the current level, and this stock could trend towards $2/share if there is no material recovery in membership and ad revenue growth.

IQ’s membership revenue declined by 15% y/y to RMB 4.1bn due to a lighter content slate. Management did note a sequential recovery in Q1 membership revenue driven by new content such as Drifting Away and Moonlight Mystique, but even if there is a sequential recovery, we expect the membership revenue to continue to decline in the high single-digit percentage on a y/y basis.

More concerning is the lack of full-year guidance as we wrap up two months of 2025, implying incremental uncertainty towards the membership revenue trajectory for the year. Instead, management provided some direction on the content lineup for 2025 by emphasizing the upcoming premium dramas including Breaking the Shadows, Justifiable Defense, A Life for a Life, Low IQ Crime, Fox Spirit Matchmaker: Sword and Beloved, A Dream Within a Dream, and What A Wonderful World. In addition, suspense and female-led drama will be two key categories as management thinks these programs could drive higher engagement.

For mini-dramas, IQ secured partnerships with 95% of the market's leading content providers and enhanced the ad placement system to monetize the traffic in this segment. The first mini-drama surpassed RMB 1mn in revenue in December, a small feat that underscores that this segment remains in its early days and that scalability will be a key focus going forward.

Our key concern regarding the mini-drama is that IQ may be pursuing quality over quantity to remain competitive. While this may drive near-term subscriber and revenue growth, long-term dilution of quality could ultimately revert to membership decline due to the proliferation of similar dramas on other platforms such as Tencent, ByteDance, and Kuaishou. We note that competition from ByteDance is heating up, with Douyin rolling out its own subscription model for mini-dramas priced as low as RMB 8/month and access to 4 K mini-dramas. Tencent is also focused on this area, given that Tencent Video is one of the leading OTA platforms in China. The proliferation of mini-dramas is likely to erode IQ’s membership revenue as subscribers churn, and this poses a key risk to IQ’s revenue growth for 2025.

On advertising, IQ reported an ad revenue decline of 13% y/y due to weakness in brand advertising, partially offset by growth in performance ads. Management stated that brand advertising showed double-digit sequential growth in Q4, supported by premium dramas. Still, the overall sector remains challenging due to competition from platforms such as Tencent, RedNote and Bilibili.

Similar to the other advertising platforms, this segment is subject to China’s macro backdrop. Without a material recovery in the economy, this segment could remain pressured as advertisers become increasingly conscious of the ad budget amid weak business and consumer sentiment.

All things considered, we don't believe that investors should buy into IQ after the post quarter sell-off. Our preference within the online streaming platform remains Netflix over IQ as we believe that IQ faces structural challenges that prevent it from establishing a self-reinforcing content and subscription model akin to Netflix.

While Netflix has successfully built a flywheel effect, where strong content drives subscriber growth, leading to higher revenue and reinvestment into more high-quality content, iQIYI struggles to achieve the same for several reasons:

First, IQ operates within China, where the company is faced with much tougher content restrictions and regulatory oversight compared with NFLX, which does not face similar restrictions.

Second, NFLX can leverage its global audience with a higher ARPU. IQ operates in a hypercompetitive domestic Chinese market with increasing price elasticity due to consumption downgrade and sentiment that is limiting IQ’s ARPU upside.

Third, unlike NFLX, which has moved into a hybrid subscription-advertising model with premium advertisers, iQIYI remains more dependent on ad revenues, which are under pressure due to China’s weak macroeconomic conditions, as we stated above. While NFLX has seen its ad-supported tiers enhance its monetization and flywheel effect, IQ is facing tougher competition from the likes of Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) and Bilibili (BILI) when attracting high-value advertisers.

Finally, IQ remains heavily dependent on the Chinese market, with only moderate success in overseas expansion compared with NFLX, which has built a global subs base with a diversifying revenue stream. Without substantial global reach, iQIYI lacks the scalability needed to fuel a long-term flywheel effect, in our view.

Given the number of challenges, we believe a 6.5x P/E or 0.7x EV/Revenue is a fair valuation for IQ based on a blended content and ad peer. This implies further downside to the current level and is best avoided, in our view.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Palantir Drops On Defense Cut Memo
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Palantir (PLTR) ends session in red as Hegseth memo calls for sharp spending cut. (00:23) Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) to deliver more planes this year, Spirit Aero (SPR) hurdles delay A350 freighter. (01:10) Amazon (AMZN) gives up on its Chime communications service. (02:31)

This is an abridged transcript.

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) fell more than 10% on Wednesday and is down nearly 2% in premarket action.

This after a memo obtained by the Washington Post from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called for an 8% cut to the defense budget over each of the next five years.

The news outlet added that the proposed cuts are required to be drawn up by Feb. 24. There are 17 categories that are said to be exempted from the cuts, including security at the southern U.S. border, the modernization of the U.S. nuclear weapon arsenal and missile defense, one-way attack drones and other areas.

News of the potential cuts were first reported over the weekend.

Palantir did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

—

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) is expecting to deliver more planes in 2025 compared to a year ago even as it faces continued supply chain challenges and broader uncertainty in light of global trade tensions.

The European aircraft maker expects to deliver around 820 commercial planes this year, slightly lower than the consensus estimate of 824 jets, but higher than 766 deliveries in 2024.

The outlook assumes no additional disruptions to global trade, air traffic, the supply chain, and Airbus' (OTCPK:EADSF) internal operations. It also excludes the impact of potential new tariffs on the company's business.

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) pushed the entry-into-service date for the A350 freighter variant to the second half of 2027, from its earlier target of 2026.

The company continues to target a monthly production rate of 12 A350s in 2028 and 14 A220s in 2026. The A320 family continues to ramp up towards a rate of 75 aircraft per month in 2027, while monthly A330 production is stabilizing at around rate of 4.

The plane maker is taking over some parts of Spirit Aerosystems (SPR) business, while rival Boeing (BA) is acquiring the majority of the business.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is giving up on its Chime communication service.

The company announced on Wednesday that it is withdrawing support for the Amazon Chime service next year and will stop accepting new customers today.

Organizations with at least one Amazon Chime Team or an enterprise account can continue to use Amazon Chime and Business Calling features until February 20, 2026, when all support for the service will end.

Amazon Chime was officially announced on February 13, 2017 and positioned itself as a competitor to established services like Skype for Business, Cisco WebEx, Google Hangouts, and LogMeIn's GoToMeeting.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Beyond Meat in talks with lenders to help shore up its liquidity - Bloomberg

Federal Reserve officials consider trade, immigration impacts: FOMC minutes

Carbon capture would boost U.S. energy independence, Occidental CEO says

Catalyst watch:

  • The CAGNY Conference will include presentations from Procter & Gamble (PG), US Foods (USFD), Diageo (DEO), and Clorox (CLX).

  • Walmart (WMT) will hold its earnings conference call. The retail giant's business updates have impacted shares of Target (TGT), Costco (COST), Dollar General (DG), Dollar Tree (DLTR), and Kroger (KR) in the past. Major suppliers with a strong post-earnings trading correlation with Walmart include Reynolds Consumer Products (REYN), TreeHouse Foods (THS), and Flower Foods (FLO).

  • Mobileye Global (MBLY) will present at the Barclays Industrial Select Conference.

  • Boeing (BA) CEO Kelly Ortberg will speak at the Barclays Industrial Select Conference.

  • Visa (V) will hold its Investor Day event.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $72/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1% at $97,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.3% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Shares of Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) dropped around 10% despite a strong Q4 performance, which saw the company beat top- and bottom-line estimates, increase car sales, and improve profit per vehicle.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 9:35 am Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Chicagoland Chamber Mid-Market Chicago event.

  • 12:05 p.m St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank President Alberto Musalem will speak and participate in a moderated conversation on the U.S. economy and monetary policy before the Economic Club of New York.

  • 2:30 pm Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr will speak on "Supervision and Regulation" at the Georgetown University Law Center.

  • 5:00 pm Federal Reserve Board Governor Adriana Kugler will speak on "Navigating Inflation Waves While Riding on the Phillips Curve" before the 2025 Whittington Lecture event hosted by Georgetown University.

data.title: India Gold Market Update: Record High Prices, Accompanied By Investment Interest
data.description:
Looking ahead

  • Gold investment interest is expected to remain strong, even as jewellery demand faces pressure from record-high prices. The financial year-end dynamics, which include statutory payments and tax-saving investments, may curtail discretionary spending, further weighing down demand. However, price stability could be a mitigating factor for jewellery demand, which could see an improvement in the new fiscal year starting in April.

Gold achieves a new peak

Heading into 2025, gold has not only reversed the price moderation seen in November-December (a decline of 6%), but it has also repeatedly hit new -record highs. So far in 2025,1 the LBMA gold price AM in USD has surged by US$286/oz or 10% to US$2,938/oz. Domestic prices have been rising in parallel with international prices, rising by 14% to a record INR86,831/10g,2 with the higher gains attributed to the weakness in the INR against the USD (1.1% depreciation y-t-d). Our analysis indicates that the upward climb in gold prices can be attributed to a combination of geopolitical risks, growing concerns about inflation, and increased investment flows.

Chart 1: Gold breaks previous records

LBMA Price AM and domestic landed price by month, US$ and INR*

Union Budget 2025-26: key highlights pertaining to gold

  • One of the key takeaways from the Union budget presented on 1 February for gold is that the import duty hasn’t been changed. In the run-up to the budget there were worries that the government might hike the duty due to the rise in gold imports after it reduced the duty by 9% back in July 2024
  • On the other hand, the government cut the customs tariff on gold jewellery from 25% to 20%. This is likely done as part of the overall rationalisation of tariffs across commodities. However, since jewellery imports aren’t that significant and are limited to high-end jewellery (and of low caratage), this cut in duty is unlikely to have much impact on domestic jewellery production
  • It was also announced that new tariff lines will be introduced under the HSN codes3 for precious metal from 1 May to distinguish imports of precious metals in various forms. The new tariff lines will differentiate gold imports in bar form from other types. This is done to address the disruptions caused by imports of gold in forms such as platinum alloy and gold paste. From May, the tariff rates can differ based on the new classification
  • The government has also decided not to issue any sovereign gold bonds as part of its market borrowing programme. This could work in favour of gold ETFs, as investors looking for gold-related financial products may turn to ETFs instead.

Price surge takes shine out of jewellery demand, maintains investment interest

The rally in gold prices to repeated new all-time highs since the start of the year has weighed heavily on the retail demand for gold jewellery. Uncertainty about announcements in the Union Budget also influenced buying activity.

Anecdotal reports indicate that demand dropped sharply in January and the weakness persisted into February, despite the end of the inauspicious period in the Hindu calendar (15 Dec - 15 Jan) and the usual-post Union Budget pick-up in demand. Wedding-related purchases too have been subdued, suggesting that many consumers had front loaded their purchases when prices dipped in November. Rather than making fresh purchases, many buyers are opting to exchange old gold for new jewellery. Additionally, as gold prices surged past previous thresholds, many consumers are also taking the opportunity to sell old gold and lock in profits.

This slowdown in jewellery demand has left retailers reluctant to restock, as they face challenges in meeting payment terms with manufacturers. This has created a liquidity crunch within the industry. The subdued demand environment was reflected in the widening spread between domestic and international prices. Since December, domestic gold prices3 have been trading at a discount to international prices, with the gap widening from an average US$3/oz in December to US$23/oz.4

Notwithstanding the depressed jewellery demand, investment demand interest (for bars and coins) has stayed the course with investors anticipating further price increases.

Record inflows into gold ETFs

2025 began with strong interest in Indian gold ETFs, marked by unprecedented inflows in January. According to the Association of Mutual Funds in India (AMFI), gold ETFs recorded net inflows of INR37.5bn(~US$435mn) in January, significantly higher than the average inflows of INR9.4bn(~US$112mn) over the previous 12 months. The cumulative assets under management (AUM) of gold ETFs grew to INR51.8bn(~US$6bn), an 15% m/m increase and 4.6t were added to the overall holdings, taking the collective holdings to 62.4t. These figures are close to our initial estimates, which were based on information available at the time.5

Anecdotal reports suggest that the strong inflows in January can be attributed to investors redirecting free cash flow towards gold ETFs for diversification amid ongoing global and domestic economic and policy uncertainty. The sustained weakness in the domestic equity markets has also been driving flows into gold ETFs, with investors pulling back from equities in favour of the safe-haven appeal of gold.

In February, a new product was launched, bringing the total number of gold ETFs in India to 19,6 highlighting the strong momentum in this space.

Chart 2: Inflows soar

Monthly gold ETF fund flows in INRbn, and total holdings in tonnes*

Gold buying resumes at the RBI after a brief pause

The RBI resumed its gold purchases in January, after pausing in December following 11 consecutive months of buying. The central bank added 2.8t of gold to its gold holding during the month, taking its total gold reserves to a new high of 879t. This renewed buying suggests that the RBI is likely to continue with its gold accumulation, following a significant purchase of 72.6t in 2024, making it the third largest buyer of gold among global central banks that year.

Not only is the RBI building its gold reserves, the share of gold in its forex reserves has been steadily climbing from 7.7% in January 2024 to 11.31% by early February 2025.7 This increase reflects the RBI’s efforts to diversify its forex reserves, alongside a decline in its holding of foreign currency assets (from 88.5% to 85.2%).

Chart 3: RBI’s gold holdings rise

RBI’s net purchases and reserves, in tonnes*

Gold imports slow in January

Gold imports in January saw a noteworthy drop owing to high prices leading the pull-back in demand. Anecdotal market reports suggest that manufacturers did not pick-up imports, reflecting the depressed demand environment. January's imports were the lowest since July 2024. According to Ministry of Commerce data, the gold import bill for the month totalled $2.68bn, a 43% decrease compared to December. However, it was approximately 40% higher than January of the previous year. We estimate that the volume of imports in January ranged between 30t-35t.

Chart 4: Gold imports cool from peak

Monthly gold imports in tonnes and US$bn*

Footnotes

1As of 14 February, 2025.

2Based on the landed price of gold (international prices adjusted for import taxes and exchange rate) as of 14 February, 2025.

3HSN (Harmonized System of Nomenclature) code is a system used to classify goods in international trade and is accepted globally.

4Domestic gold prices refer to the landed prices, which are the international prices adjusted for import taxes and exchange rate.

5 As of 14 February, 2025.

6The daily AUM and NAV data published by AMFI covers 15 of the country’s 18 gold ETFs.

7As of 14 February, 2025.

8As of 7 February, 2025.

data.title: USA Compression: Edging Closer To Positive Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Thesis

USA Compression (NYSE:USAC) reported full year earnings last week in addition to 2025 guidance. The company reported record EBITDA generation in the fourth quarter, aided by the continuing trend of rising revenue per deployed horsepower.

In the near term, the business outlook looks to be stable with the potential to add momentum at the end of 2025 through the delivery of four new large horsepower compression units. Over the medium term, the company is positioned to improve its financial position by riding the wave of a rising natural gas compression market.

The timing of this wave is advantageous, as the company has few internal options for growth. Approximately 30% of 2025 EBITDA is expected to be used to finance interest expenses, thereby restricting USAC's ability to deploy growth capital. USAC has again cut its CAPEX budget in an effort to become free cash flow positive.

USAC's projected growth CAPEX for 2025 is roughly half that of its peers. In time, this will lead to a falling market share and lower spec equipment compared to its more aggressive (and lower leveraged) peers.

I rate USAC as a HOLD due to a "rising tides lift all boats" thought process combined with a high yield of 7.4%. These factors offset the company's disadvantaged financial position.

4th Quarter Update For USAC

I last covered USAC with a HOLD rating in September with the title "Steady As She Goes, But Lacking In Total Returns". In that article, I discussed how USAC's 10-year performance has essentially mirrored that of the S&P 500 from a total returns perspective. The returns simply came in the form of yield versus capital appreciation.

The negatives I expressed at the time were that the company's growth opportunities were losing momentum while the firm also remained cash flow negative. This effectively limited USAC from capitalizing on many available growth prospects because it could not invest capital at the same rates as its peers.

Since that article, not much has changed operationally. For the full year the company has grown EBITDA to $584 million, a healthy 14% since I started covering the company in late 2023. The bulk of this growth was derived from general market strength that has allowed revenue per horsepower to grow 8% YOY. The remainder is from a 6% increase in total deployed horsepower.

For the full year of 2024, USAC generated negative free cash flow after paying the company's distribution. This has contributed to the company's steadily increasing debt levels. Interest expenses for the year totaled $185 million, or 30% of EBITDA.

As I alluded to in my previous article, the debt levels are not horrific but severely limit the company's options if it ever wants to achieve positive free cash flow. After a year of investing CAPEX into revamping older equipment for redeployment, the low-hanging fruit now appears picked with the company's total utilization factor now sitting around 95%.

New Management Is Tightening The Belt

Since my last analysis of USAC, the company has appointed a new CEO and CFO. The two new executives indicated that in the near term they would have a measured response towards growth in a way that would not negatively affect the partnership's debt metrics.

Chris Paulsen - USAC CFO - Q4 2024 Conference Call

Right now, that is the focus, at least as it relates to our growth capital in 2025 is make sure that the relative standing and relative measures and debt measures of the business are not impacted in a significant way, especially as it relates to the ability to go out and refinance some of our fixed notes. So that's the near-term view for me in managing the business.

In the near term, this has forced the company to cut the 2025 CAPEX budget to roughly half that spent in 2024. Despite multiple growth initiatives for natural gas, USAC has little choice if it ever intends to be FCF positive. The bulk of the 2025 CAPEX is expected to be deployed in the form of four new large horsepower units totaling 10,000 HP. These are expected to be deployed in Q4, 2025.

Free Cash Flow Analysis

The cost cutting efforts by USAC's new management goes a long way to improving the cash flows of the business. Below is the comparison between 2024 results and 2025 guidance. Here we can see that the reduced CAPEX and increased EBITDA projects to generate a near breakeven cash flow position in 2025.

2024 2025e
EBITDA $584 million $600 million
Interest Expense ($193) million ($185) million
Distribution Expense ($265) million ($265) million
Capital Expense (net) ($275) million ($170 million)
Net Cash From Operations ($149) million ($20) million

Taking this view, we can see that 2026 is likely to be free cash flow positive as EBITDA continues to grow through industry wide revenue/horsepower growth. Unfortunately, this looks to be the company's only meaningful growth driver. Meaningful amounts of capital would be required to grow a fleet with 3.5 million horsepower already deployed. 10,000 of new HP is really just a drop in the bucket for a fleet of this size.

This means that USAC is simply treading water and rising with the tide. Cash flows are simply too tight to make any meaningful changes to debt levels or bring on new units.

Caught Up In AI Growth

Despite muted growth prospects, the company's stock has gotten caught up in the market's frenzy for anything associated with AI. This has created a 30% total return in a relatively short period of time. It has also created an abrupt deviation from the company's historical valuation as shown below.

The simplest rationale here is that "rising tides lift all boats". There is clear merit to this thought process after 8% growth in revenue per operating horsepower through 2024. However, the stock has already priced in all of the growth expected in 2025.

The company's guidance for $600 million in EBITDA in 2025 places the forward EV:EBITDA ratio at 9.73x. This is essentially in line with the company's historical average. With a full year's worth of growth already priced in, and minimal capital investment from the company, the tide must continue to rise for compression units for any future growth in 2026 to be expected.

In my previous assessment, I showed how USAC was able to keep up with the S&P 500 through its distribution. This is possible through a double-digit average yield over the last decade. However, the current 7.4% yield is at a decade low with no room for distribution growth (as shown above).

This leads me to conclude that USAC's competitors Kodiak Gas Services (KGS) and Archrock (AROC) may prove to have better total returns through this bull run in natural gas production. So far this year, both of these stocks have more than doubled USAC's total return (including the recent 30% gain).

Both AROC and KGS are investing larger amounts of capital to capture future growth. Additionally, while USAC was refurbishing older equipment in 2024, KGS and AROC were deploying top spec equipment that are likely to generate a higher margin. Both of these facts could cause USAC to lose market share over time.

Risks

By rising with the tide, the company will likely be in fine financial shape in the short and medium terms. However, if the tide were to roll out, USAC would be significantly less attractive. A black swan event could easily tip the scales and push the company back into negative free cash flows.

After already cutting CAPEX spending, there aren't many levers left to pull without touching the distribution. The company did not cut to preserve cash flows in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic so it would appear that USAC and its parent, Energy Transfer (ET) are intent on preserving this stream of cash flow for the larger entity.

A theoretical event such as this would likely seal the deal for no distribution growth for the remainder of the decade, making USAC dead money in a contracting market.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. USAC was able to grow 2024 EBITDA through a combination of rising market rates for available compression and the redeployment of idle units.

2. The company remained cash flow negative in 2024.

3. The company's new CEO and CFO have indicated they plan to drive toward positive free cash flow.

4. This will be primarily achieved through reduced capital spending, leaving the rising tide of the compression market as the main source of EBITDA growth.

5. The recent gain in unit price has priced in 2025 EBITDA growth compared to historical valuations. It has also compressed yields to a decade low.

6. This will make it difficult for USAC to continue to match the performance of the S&P 500. However, the steady and reliable yield allows me to continue to rate USAC as a HOLD.

data.title: QGRW: A Bet On Technology And The U.S. Largest Companies
data.description:
The Wisdomtree U.S. Quality Growth Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:QGRW) is a fund with a strong focus on larger capitalization companies with a growth profile, while also favoring high-quality companies.

Although the fund’s investment strategy has worked over its nearly two years of operation, its relatively high valuations and above-average volatility are somewhat concerning. That said, the big question is whether this fund can continue to deliver going forward, supported by generally good prospects for the fund’s top holdings and technology, which is by far its largest sector.

ETF Description & Highlights

QGRW is an ETF that provides exposure to U.S. large- and mid-capitalization companies with above-average quality and growth profiles. This fund follows the Wisdomtree U.S. Quality Growth Index, which is designed to select 100 companies from an initial universe of the 500 largest U.S. companies by market cap.

According to the index’s methodology, companies are assigned a composite score that equally weights growth and quality measures. A growth score is calculated by considering a 50% weighting from median analyst earnings growth forecasts, alongside 25% from the trailing five-year EBITDA growth and 25% from the trailing five-year sales growth. Meanwhile, a quality score relies on return on equity and return on assets over the past three years, each contributing 50% to the quality score. The index then selects the top 100 companies according to the composite score and weights them based on their market capitalization.

As of February 14, 2025, QGRW had $1.1 billion in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 100 companies, with an average market cap of $602.2 billion. The allocation is skewed to mega caps, which represent 63.8% of total assets, followed by large caps at 20.0% and mid-caps at 16.2%. This figure illustrates a slight shift toward mega caps since my previous article covering QGRW last May, as allocation to mega caps increased from 59.1% to 63.8% during this period, which is consistent with the fund’s average market cap growth from $444.1 billion to $602.2 billion.

A comparison with the Russell 1000 index, a large- and mid-cap benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), shows the Russell 1000 index with a more balanced allocation across different market-cap sizes, as the benchmark exposure to mega caps accounts for 42.7%, while large caps represent 32.9% of the index.

QGRW’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA (10.73%), Apple (10.66%), Microsoft (8.63%), Alphabet (5.82%), Amazon (5.64%), Meta Platforms (5.1%), Broadcom (4.06%), Tesla (3.23%), Eli Lilly (3.0%), and Visa (2.83%), which together represent 60% of total assets, with a heavy participation of the technology sector (NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, Broadcom), alongside leaders in communication services (Alphabet, Meta Platforms) and consumer discretionary (Amazon, Tesla).

The following table includes a list of growth-oriented funds that will serve as the benchmark for QGRW in the analysis. The first three funds (QGRO, FTGS, and FAD) also have a bias toward quality. The first, the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO), screens stocks based on growth and quality factors from an initial universe consisting of the 1,000 largest U.S. companies. The next fund is the First Trust Growth Strength ETF (FTGS), which applies growth and fundamental metrics to select 50 companies that are equally weighted, promoting a balanced exposure. The last one, the First Trust Multi Cap Growth AlphaDEX ETF (FAD), also identifies stocks based on growth and fundamental factors, such as price appreciation, sales growth, and sales-to-price, and employs a tiered weighting strategy across different market cap segments.

The second group consists of typical growth funds (IWF, IUSG, and SCHG). The first, the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), one of the largest funds in the category, managing $106 billion, is a subset of the Russell 1000 index, while the iShares Core S&P U.S. Growth ETF (IUSG) is a market cap-weighted fund based on the S&P 900 index. The last fund is the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), which selects stocks from the largest 750 companies according to various fundamental metrics.

From a sector perspective, QGRW’s largest allocation is to the technology sector, with 52.4% of total equities, followed by consumer discretionary at 15.5%, communication services at 13.4%, financial services at 7.7%, healthcare at 6.2%, industrials at 2.9%, energy at 1.1%, consumer staples at 0.4%, and utilities at 0.5%. The fund has no exposure to real estate or basic materials. The fund’s sector allocation is roughly the same as in my previous article on this fund in May 2024, with the largest differences consisting of an increase in exposure to consumer discretionary from 14.3% to 15.5% and a reduction in communication services from 14.9% to 13.4%.

Relative to the Russell 1000 index, QGRW is heavily overweight in technology (+21.0%), consumer discretionary (+4.3%), and communication services (+3.7%), but underweight in financial services (-6.1%), industrials (-5.1%), and consumer staples (-5.1%). The fund’s large allocation to technology is highly concentrated in software and semiconductors, making up nearly 33% of the total fund, with overweight exposure to the sector’s largest players, such as Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Apple. Meanwhile, QGRW’s positions in consumer discretionary and communication services are similarly concentrated in the largest companies like Amazon, Tesla, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, and Netflix.

On the other hand, QGRWs' underweight allocation to financial services is driven by virtually no exposure to banks and insurers, with the largest holdings coming from consumer finance (Visa) and asset management (Blackstone and Ares Management).

When compared to the peer group, QGRW is overweight in technology (+14.1%), consumer discretionary (+2.3%), and communication services (+2.2%), as the fund has the largest allocation to technology among peers, while quality-oriented funds like FTGS and FAD have a rather conservative approach to this sector, with an allocation of less than 30%. On the other hand, QGRW is underweight in industrials (-6.1%), financial services (-4.5%), and consumer staples (-2.7%), driven by FTGS and FAD’s above-average exposure to industrials and financial service, while growth funds IWF and SCHG, similar to QGRW, also have a limited allocation to these two sectors.

High Valuations

QGRW's average price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) of 31.7x represents a 42.3% premium over the Russell 1000 index, as the fund’s valuation multiple increased nearly 11.1% from 28.5x in May 2024 to its current levels, while the Russell 1000 index P/E ratio increased by a smaller 7.5%. This additional premium over the benchmark reflects the outperformance of the fund’s largest holdings, in particular big techs, relative to the overall market. By and large, QGRW’s higher valuation relative to the Russell 1000 index is driven, of course, by its overweight allocation to the technology sector, but also by higher P/E ratios across the board, especially in holdings from technology and consumer discretionary, such as Tesla (P/E 173.7x), NVIDIA (P/E 46.4x), and Apple (P/E 34.6x) to name a few.

The fund’s earnings growth is higher than that of the Russell 1000 index by at least two percentage points, due to stronger growth in technology stocks, such as NVIDIA (EPS 3Y growth of 97.4%), and GoDaddy (EPS 3Y growth of 101.6%) and consumer discretionary, led by holdings in Tesla (EPS 3Y growth of 52.5%). On the profitability side, QGRW’s average EBITDA margin of 37.8% is five percentage points higher than the Russell 1000 index, given higher EBITDA margins in most sectors, including technology (e.g., Microsoft: 53.7%), communication services (e.g., Meta Platforms: 50.7%), financial services (e.g., Visa: 69.5%), and healthcare.

QGRW’s P/E ratio is the highest in the peer group, with three funds (QGRO, IWF, and SCHG) trading in the range of P/E 29 x to 30x, as their sector exposure does not offer a relevant valuation does not offer a relevant valuation gap. Meanwhile, funds blending growth and quality approaches show much lower valuations, with FTGS’s P/E at 20.8x and FAD’s P/E at 23.8x, as these funds have less exposure to the technology sector, but also due to their stock selection methodologies, which represent nearly two-thirds of the valuation gap.

Strong Returns

QGRW has a track record of roughly two years, as its inception was on Dec 13, 2022. After a strong year in 2023, the fund outperformed again in 2024, surpassing the Russell 1000 index by 5% during the period. QGRW was also the best fund in 2023 and remained among the top three in 2024, alongside IUSG and SCHG, which positions QGRW as one of the best growth funds, while it is by far the strongest performer among those that incorporate quality into their investment strategies, such as QGRO, FTGS, and FAD.

On the other hand, QGRW’s volatility has been slightly higher than the Russell 1000 index, with its beta for a one-year period at 1.08. The same applies when comparing it with its peers, as QGRW’s beta has been just above funds IWF, IUSG, and SCHG, Meanwhile, paradoxically, quality-oriented funds have generated higher volatility during the same stretch, despite displaying generally similar betas over longer time frames compared to pure growth funds IWF, IUSG, and SCHG.

QGRW is a liquid fund with an average trading volume of $10.3 million, which is similar to other quality-growth funds, such as QGRO and FTGS, although much lower than growth funds in the peer group. Meanwhile, QGRW’s average spread of 0.04% is in the range of the most liquid funds, while quality-growth funds have seen spreads between 0.15% and 0.20%. On the other hand, its expense ratio of 0.28% is in the middle of the pack, lower than FTGS and FAD at nearly 0.60% level, but much higher than growth funds. Meanwhile, its turnover of 27% is the third lowest in the peer group. QGRW’s dividend yield of 0.14% is the lowest in the group, reflecting the fund’s low exposure to utilities, real estate, and energy. Lastly, QGRW’s tracking error of 7% is just above the peer group average, while its adjusted returns, as measured by its Sharpe ratio, are dragged down by the fund’s relatively higher volatility, despite strong returns in 2024.

Final Thoughts

QGRW’s methodology has a strong bias toward growth. After all, its heavy allocation to mega caps and technology positions this fund as a growth play, much like other growth funds like IWF, IUSG, and SCHG. In the meantime, its quality tilt is also evidenced by high EBITDA margins, a feature that is largely due to a combination of high margins seen in both mega caps and in the largest tech companies such as Microsoft and NVIDIA.

In addition, its outperformance during the first two years is consistent with its heavy allocation to technology, which is the largest among peers, and overweight holdings in big winners like NVIDIA, Broadcom, and Meta Platforms.

Looking ahead, given the strength of the U.S. economy, there is a good possibility that earnings growth for these high-profile names can continue to be strong, which is expected to support further gains going forward. That said, while high valuations are something to keep in mind, especially after two years of strong gains, the overall outlook remains positive for growth stocks. Thus, although I view QGRW as one of the options for investors looking for exposure to large-cap growth stocks, given the growth characteristics of its portfolio, the fund’s relatively high valuations and above-average volatility lead me to take a neutral view on QGRW compared to other growth-oriented funds.

data.title: Hertz Global: I Am Still Wary About The Near-Term Outlook
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought for Hertz Global (NASDAQ:HTZ) was a sell rating because there were visible headwinds that could impair the equity value. Following 4Q24 results, I stay sell rated. My concerns remain valid—weak revenue trends, elevated DPU, and a potential liquidity risk should continue to weigh on the stock price. While management is working to improve profitability through fleet optimization, the near-term headwinds outweigh the positives.

4Q24 results were not great

HTZ delivered a weak 4Q24. Revenue came in at $2.04 billion, declining 6.6% y/y, which reflects both the impact of having a smaller fleet and soft rental demand. Hence, EBITDA came in a lot weaker than expected, at -$357 million vs. consensus estimates of -$123 million. The key cause was an elevated depreciation per unit [DPU] of $422, which was well above management’s guided range of $350-$375. While management remains focused on improving profitability through a newer vehicle mix, my view (as I will talk about below) is that the near-term outlook remains challenging.

Fleet Reduction May Limit Revenue Growth

The key reason for me being bearish on HTZ's ability to rejuvenate growth is because the business is still in the process of right-sizing its fleet. While this aligns with management’s effort to optimize asset utilization, the cost of doing so is that it constrains HTZ’s ability to capture demand growth, especially if leisure or business travel picks up. My logic is that smaller fleet means lesser rental capacity, which should result in lower revenue growth (or even negative growth). In the 4Q24 quarter, average fleet size declined 4% y/y, resulting in FY24 seeing a 7% y/y decline.

The bigger problem is that HTZ may lose its competitive positioning. While HTZ is streamlining fleet size and operations, competitors like Avis Budget Group (CAR) appear to have maintained a more flexible approach in their fleet strategy. If demand unexpectedly rebounds (for whatever reason), HTZ may not have the capacity to capture this rebound, and CAR would swoop in to win market share.

As always, our goal is to be disciplined in aligning our fleet size with demand driving higher utilizations in the first-quarter and throughout the year. CAR 4Q24 earnings

Used-Car Market Uncertainty Poses a Risk to Depreciation Costs

In terms of profitability, I think the biggest challenge for HTZ is how the used-car market will develop (or rather how the used car price will trend). While management expects DPU to decline throughout 2025, the underlying assumption is that vehicle residual values will see some form of stabilization and not go down. Suppose the macro environment remains poor, especially if interest rates stay high (which indirectly impacts demand for cars, and therefore drives down residual value), DPU could remain elevated longer than what management expects. Do not forget that HTZ is also actively working to reduce its fleet of electric vehicles [EVs], and residual values of EVs have continued to decline (the price of the Tesla Model 3 has especially fallen hard). Given that EV prices remain a hurdle for consumers, demand could remain poor, causing residual prices to continue declining, and HTZ DPU for its EV fleets could go up.

Liquidity and Debt Refinancing Remain an Overhang

I am also concerned about HTZ’s balance sheet. HTZ ended 4Q24 with $1.8 billion in liquidity, including $592 million in cash and $1.25 billion in revolver availability. While this provides flexibility over the near-term, this does not remove the concern of poor cash flow. Note that management made it clear that HTZ is unlikely to start building cash until 3Q25, so for the next three quarters, HTZ still could face liquidity concerns if the operating environment gets worse.

We don't expect to start building cash until the third quarter and we'll use cash in our fleet rotation until the first during the first and second quarter of this year. 4Q24 earnings transcript

The real problem is when HTZ enters 2026, when a portion of HTZ debt matures. Ultimately, the credit and equity markets look at both the quantity and quality of business cash flow to make financing decisions. If EBITDA remains weak through 2025, it could be very hard for HTZ to refinance this portion of the debt. HTZ could pay off this entire debt with its available liquidity today, but that would mean it only has ~$1.1 billion to turn things around this year (FY24 FCF was -$1.78 billion for reference). It would not be impossible, but I think it will be very difficult as management is constrained by the lack of financial flexibility.

Valuation

The market is evidently pricing an improvement in profitability for HTZ, given that it is valued at 35x forward EBITDA (one-year forward) and 11x on a two-year forward basis (this is a better metric since it is more normalized). I don’t discredit the potential for HTZ to improve profitability, but I think the premium to where CAR trades at is too much. CAR trades at ~8x two-year forward EBITDA, a ~3x discount to HTZ. Considering that CAR is in a better position with no fleet and potential liquidity issues, I believe HTZ should trade at a discount rather than a premium. Suppose things do not get better for HTZ in the next few quarters; I expect valuation to come down to below CAR.

Upside risk

While I noted HTZ faces challenges, if used-car prices stabilize or rebound, DPU could decline faster than expected, which boosts profitability. Additionally, if fleet utilization improves and pricing gets better, revenue could recover even with a smaller fleet. Liquidity risks will also go away if free cash flow turns positive (due to the improvements in the operating environment).

Conclusion

My view for HTZ is a sell rating as HTZ continues to face challenges. Management’s strategy to reduce DPU could improve profitability, but I am not sure if this will play out as expected. In addition, there is a potential liquidity risk if cash flow generation does not pick up. Valuation also appears expensive in my view, relative to CAR. If there is no improvement in HTZ fundamentals, I expect the market to downgrade HTZ’s multiple to below CAR.

data.title: 3 Years On, I'm Upgrading AGGH To Buy
data.description:
Introduction

On Valentine's Day, three years ago, Simplify debuted the Simplify Aggregate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:AGGH). This fund was designed to do one thing: maximize returns. This is a little vague, and has led to a lot of weird looking allocations over time. The fund is constantly evolving and changing as it has a go at this mission.

Simplify's version is a little more wordy:

The [fund] seeks to maximize total return. The fund is actively managed to create a core bond exposure with enhanced yield via structural income opportunities such as more efficient option writing and curve positioning. AGGH can be used by investors who not only seek higher yields than investment grade bonds normally provide, but a higher total return as well.

What is very clear is that AGGH has done its job. While it has underperformed in price, AGGH investors have had a positive return since its inception compared to the index's negative return.

The fund has also been able to pump out considerable income, with yields over 10% at several points last year, and a current trailing yield of 8.56% compared to the index's 3.76%.

I initiated coverage on this fund in October 2023, and gave it a hold rating. I called it "complex," and I lamented the fact that the fund's holdings were primarily made up of other Simplify products, some of which had very small AUMs and could prove illiquid if AGGH needed to expand.

Those problems have since been somewhat ironed out, and the fund today looks very different from the fund back when I first took a look at it.

Running the Experiment Live

This header comes from a comment on one of my articles; I can't remember which one it was, or who wrote it, but I do remember the interesting part of the quote:

Simplify likes to run their experiments live.

This is one of the perceptions that folks have about Simplify funds, and one I think seems to be fairly accurate. Their actively managed funds change a lot, and their strategies have evolved significantly over time. This is something that investors need to be comfortable with. It's easier to swallow when you see outperformance in the past, but know that it doesn't indicate any future performance, and doubly so because the AGGH (or really any of Simplify's actively managed funds like SVOL) strategy may change so significantly that forecasting its performance is impossible.

When I first covered the fund in October 2023, the majority of its assets were spread across various bond ETFs, and the fund had a credit spread options overlay. It has significant exposure to bond futures as well, upwards of a 60% allocation to the 10yr UST.

Today, it looks very different.

I don't believe this is a bad thing, per se. It just needs to be a consideration for investors; some will be far more keen to let the managers run the experiment, and others would prefer to own funds that have a set strategy and are past the experimenting stage.

Let's take a look at what it owns now.

AGGH Holdings

Today's holdings are very different and much more concise. There are three moving parts I can identify from looking over today's holdings.

These parts all move separately, but contribute to AGGH's overall performance. As I see them, the pieces are:

  1. The Core
  2. Bond Futures Strategy
  3. Interest Rate Swap Strategy

The core is AGGH's primary allocation to bonds. While in the past, it held various bond ETFs that represented the different classes of bonds, today it holds the index outright at a 94.78% allocation. It's unclear to me whether Simplify did this because they believe there are no opportunities in mispricing between market weights (as they are held in AGG), or for liquidity's sake.

After all, their AUM is now over $200M, and they need a fair bit of liquidity in the underlying assets they trade. To note, this is up over 150% from when I first covered AGGH.

The second moving part is the bond futures strategy, which is a significantly smaller portion of NAV now than it was before. This is aimed at targeting mispricing along the yield curve. As rates are expected to normalize over the next few years, bonds today may be mis-priced based on a mismatch to the reality of what rates will actually be. This means that taking positions along the futures curve may prove to be very beneficial if it reacts how the managers expect it to.

Right now, they are long the 10YR note and short the 30YR bond. This also manipulates the duration of the fund, allowing it to raise or lower its overall duration dynamically using the futures.

Here is the current yield curve, for reference.

This goes hand-in-hand with the last moving part, which has replaced the credit spread options overlay. I'm happy about this, for the record, as it has removed the commodities and equities risks from the fund, which it suffered from when it was writing options against these assets.

Here is a better image of just the interest rate swaps:

I am not well-versed enough in interest rate swaps to really say what's going on from just this image, so I hope that someone in the comments can chime in here. What I can tell you is that this is a complex strategy that involves going short and long different interest rates, and is something that retail traders typically do not have access to.

These kinds of instruments are typically traded by commodities trading advisers, or between private funds, and so having access to them is a positive for investors here. There are few other funds on the market that give investors access to CTA-level derivatives. The primary source of returns outside of holding AGG is generated by these swaps, and there are few other ETFs that offer anything similar. This is a big standout point for AGGH.

How well they implement it is unclear to me, so I hope someone can chime in on what thesis these positions indicate Simplify is going after. There are a lot of moving parts to that piece of the equation.

This brings us to another risk: the fund is a black box. While Simplify has some good videos and explainers on its website, and they publish regular commentary about their funds, not everything is entirely explained, and some of these materials are out-of-date, explaining strategies the fund is no longer utilizing. This means that investors need to trust the managers to some degree, and that is a risk that individuals will have to weigh taking.

Suitability

AGGH in its current form seems to be a great way for investors to explore high-yield offerings with similar risks to the core bond index. AGGH has shown its ability to keep up with the index over time, and has made-up for its prior shortfalls and my previous complaints. It is no longer as dependents on its sister funds, has maintained its high payout, and still outperformed the index in total return.

This has earned an upgrade from me, and I would advise that investors consider AGGH as a compliment to a core bond holding in a portfolio. Aggressive investors could consider up to a 20% allocation to the fund, and moderate investors up to 10%. Conservative investors should still consider the index on its own, as the actively managed portion of AGGH may be outside their risk tolerance.

Personally, I have given it a weight of 15% in my all-ETF bond portfolio, and hold it as a core holding.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the Simplify Aggregate Bond ETF has impressed me with its track record, new strategies, and the fact that it's now providing investors to an asset class previously fairly inaccessible. AGGH has outperformed its index, and proven that it can provide higher total returns above the index while still maintaining a near-10% yield. The fund has earned a core spot in my own portfolio, but it is now at a point and composition where I feel I can recommend it to investors more broadly.

That means it's received an upgrade to buy.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: The Bull Case For Ethereum
data.description:
Thesis

Ethereum (ETH-USD) is a blue chip crypto, the workhorse of DeFi apps and NFT platforms. But despite having the strongest developer community and the #1 smart contract platform, some analysts are skeptical about ETH performing well in the near future.

In this article, I'll show ETH's metrics are still strong, regulatory clarity is coming, and market trends are in its favor. A catalyst like a short squeeze could trigger a massive move. We could then witness an Ethereum rally very much like Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ripple (XRP-USD) have shown in the past few months.

ETH's dominant usage metrics

Ethereum has unequivocally fulfilled its mission by enabling the creation of an amazing number of smart contracts: in Q1 2022 alone there were 1.45 million.

But it does have competition from other blockchains, with Solana (SOL-USD) emerging as the chief rival. Newer chains are attractive because they can offer faster transactions with lower costs. Solana is the clear leader for meme coins, and platforms like Pump.fun have made it easy to create new coins and rapidly deploy them. President Trump's controversial coin (TRUMP-USD) was recently launched on Solana.

The introduction of rollups and other scaling solutions is successfully addressing Ethereum transaction speed and costs. We now have many Level 2 (L2) solutions to choose from including Optimism, Arbitrum and Coinbase's (COIN-USD) Base chain -- which has seen spectacular growth since its introduction. Ethereum developers continue to improve the main chain with exotic sounding Improvement Protocols such as Proto-Danksharding (EIP-4844).

Despite other blockchains showing increased interest, ETH's existing ecosystem is vast and has a huge lead in developer activity. ETH's present position as a dominant platform for applications is secure and this will not easily change.

ETH's Exchange-Traded Fund launched in July of 2024 and has recorded lower inflows as compared to Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) ETF. Uncertainty around ETH's regulatory status could have dampened the initial uptake for these funds. However, ETF inflow is just one marker for ETH's performance and probably not the most significant. The better metrics for ETH are in the DeFi and NFT spaces where actual usage and value show high demand. ETH is currently almost $58 billion in total locked value.

Regulatory clarity is coming

The regulatory environment for all cryptocurrency is still evolving. ETH based staking, smart contracts and DeFi applications are all areas where potential regulations could have a major effect. This has created past uncertainty for both retail and institutional investors.

But as I've documented in my earlier article, Tracking Trump's Bitcoin Promises, the regulatory outlook for all crypto is much more favorable under Trump than the disastrous Biden administration. Trump is clearly pro-crypto and has directed the SEC and a special task force to develop clear regulations for the industry. If the SEC eases guidelines around staking, we could see substantial increased institutional interest.

Recently, we've seen new proposals for Ethereum ETFs to allow staking of assets.

The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) has filed with the US regulator on behalf of asset manager Grayscale, seeking approval to introduce staking in its spot Ethereum exchange-traded funds (ETFs).

If approved, Grayscale will be permitted to stake Ether within the Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHE) and the Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust ETF (ETH), as per a Feb. 14 filing with the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

Also from the same article, "This comes just days after asset manager 21Shares became the first to file for staking within its spot Ether ETF, with CBOE BZX Exchange applying to the US SEC on its behalf."

Staking would allow the fund to generate income from their ETH assets and theoretically distribute the yield to investors, making those ETFs a much more attractive investment opportunity.

Market trends

Bitcoin and other major coins including Ripple (XRP-USD) -- see my article Why I Was Wrong About Ripple for more -- have broken out while ETH is still treading water. Current price is about $2000 less than the all-time high. Mr. Market has been a bit negative about ETH and the price has shown it. However, all that can change quickly, just like it did with XRP. What could be a catalyst towards positive price action?

The amount of ETH on exchanges has been reduced to levels not seen since 2017. This could indicate a decrease in retail investors positioning coins to be ready for sale.

According to CoinDesk, "As of the week ended Feb. 4, hedge funds held a net short position of 11,341 contracts in the CME futures, according to data tracked by ZeroHedge and the Kobeissi Letter. The number has increased 40% in one week and 500% since November, according to The Kobeissi Letter."

There's been an incredible increase in the amount of ETH being shorted and positions are near record highs. Cointelegraph reports, "ETH faces strong resistance at $3,400, with $1B in shorts set for liquidation if broken."

So with hedge funds massively shorting ETH but limited supply available to cover, this could set up a short squeeze. Is it guaranteed? Of course not, but it is a possibility and something exciting to watch for in the coming weeks.

Meanwhile, let's look at where ETH has been trending since moving down from an all-time high of $4860 in November of 2021.

From a chart perspective, ETH needs to break out of this triangle. Until a definitive move occurs, we'll simply see more of the same range bound trading. Notice this chart extends to the end of 2025 -- I don't believe we'll need to wait that long, but it's possible. This end of year timeline is a bit longer than my current estimate for the top of this crypto cycle.

Conclusion

ETH is an opportunity for profit where some analysts are bearish. Bill Barhydt, CEO of Abra, makes a base case for $8,000 ETH this cycle. Galaxy Research predicts $5,500, VanEck $6,000, Bitwise $7,000, and Ryan Adams of Bankless $10,000.

In my evaluation, ETH is a sleeping giant that's range-bound until the right catalyst triggers a massive move. Then you'll see ETH rally to catch up proportionally to big brother BTC. As always in the wild world of crypto, think for yourself and only invest what you can afford to lose.
